topic: comparing diesel vs electric aerator pumps

turns.content: friend i want ask about aerator pump. my old diesel aerator making problem again. hard to start in morning, smoke a lot. every day i pull and pull the cord, you know

Sounds like your diesel aerator is on its last legs — hard starting and heavy smoke usually means worn injectors or compression rings. Repair might cost almost as much as replacing it. Are you thinking of getting another diesel, or considering switching to electric?
some farmer near me switch to electric already. they say cheaper to run. but i am not sure, you know. diesel i understand. i can fix diesel myself. electric i dont know much about

That's a fair concern — there's value in being able to fix things yourself. Here's the basic comparison:

Diesel has higher fuel cost that keeps rising, more maintenance with oil changes, filters, and belts. But it works during power outages and you know how to repair it.

Electric is much cheaper to run — maybe 50-60% less operating cost. Less maintenance, quieter, starts with a switch. But it depends completely on reliable electricity.
that is the problem. electricity here not so good. power go out maybe 2-3 time per month, you know. sometime 4-5 hour. no aerator mean shrimp die. cannot take that risk

You're right — shrimp need aeration around the clock, especially at night. A 4-5 hour outage with no aeration could be catastrophic. That's the biggest argument for diesel.

But there's a middle option: electric aerator with a small backup diesel generator just for outages. Your neighbor who switched — does he have a backup setup?
yes he use electric with small generator for backup. but generator also use diesel, you know. so what is real saving? you still buying diesel

Good question — let me put numbers on it. Running diesel aerators full-time: maybe 8-12 liters per day, that's roughly 240-360 liters per month. Electric plus backup generator, you only burn diesel during outages — maybe 5-10 liters per month total.

So if you're spending around 3-3.5 million dong per month now on diesel and maintenance, electric would cost about 1.2-1.5 million in electricity plus maybe 200,000 in generator fuel. Roughly 2 million dong savings per month. That's about 24 million per year.
24 million per year. that is real money, you know. but what about buying everything? electric aerator not cheap. and generator too

A good electric paddle wheel aerator runs about 8-15 million dong depending on size and brand. A small backup generator is another 5-7 million. So total investment maybe 13-22 million dong.

With 24 million per year savings, the whole setup pays for itself in under a year. After that it's pure savings. The math is pretty clear.

number make sense on paper. but i have to see with my own eye first, you know. i think i go look at my neighbor setup. see how it work in real life. how fast he switch to generator when power go out. paper number and real number, sometime not same thing

That's the smart way to do it. When you visit, ask him a few specific things: how long he's had the setup, what brand he bought, any breakdowns, and most importantly how fast he can get the generator running when power cuts. If the switchover takes more than 10-15 minutes, that might be a problem in the middle of the night.

Also ask about his electricity bill before and after — real numbers from a real farmer are worth more than any estimate.

good point. switchover time important. if power go out at 2am and nobody notice for 1 hour, shrimp in trouble, you know. some farmer i hear they have automatic switch. but that sound expensive and complicated. i dont trust fancy thing

Automatic transfer switches do exist — they detect the power loss and start the generator automatically. But you're right, they add cost and complexity, and if that switch fails, you're stuck with no aeration and no alarm.

A simpler approach: some farmers just wire a loud buzzer to the power line. When electricity cuts, the buzzer stops and the silence wakes them up. Low tech, cheap, reliable. Then you walk out and start the generator manually. Takes 5 minutes.

hah buzzer stop and silence wake you up. that is clever, you know. simple. i like simple solution. okay friend i visit my neighbor this week, look at his setup. my diesel still running so no rush. but the number, 24 million per year, that is hard to ignore

The numbers are hard to ignore, and your diesel is still working so you have time to make the right decision. Go see the neighbor's setup running with your own eyes, ask the real questions, and then decide. No need to rush into anything. Good luck with the visit!
